summaryTemplate: {{className}} is the {{patternRole}}. It exposes the interface the caller expects and translates each call into one against the wrapped adaptee. Pattern body lives at {{file}}:{{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}.
chosenRationaleTemplate: Tagged as Adapter because {{className}} holds an instance of a different type and re-exposes its functionality through a different (caller-friendly) interface. The wrapper here exists for *compatibility* — to make an existing class usable where a different signature is expected — not to extend or replace its behavior.

adapter_class.note: Adapter class — owns the mapping between two interfaces. Callers see only the target interface; the adaptee underneath is hidden.

adapter_wrapped_target.note: Adaptee field — the original class the adapter is translating for. The adapter never owns or modifies this class; it only routes calls into it.

adapter_forwarding_op.note: Adapter operation — what the *caller* sees. The signature here is shaped to match what the calling code expects, even when the adaptee underneath uses a different one.

adapter_forwarded_call.note: Forwarded call — translates the caller's request into the adaptee's vocabulary. The translation may be a direct delegation or include argument reshaping; either way, no algorithmic change happens here.

runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Decorator: Decorator wraps an object that already satisfies the target interface, in order to add behavior. Adapter wraps an object whose interface is *different* from the target; the goal is compatibility, not augmentation.
runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Proxy: Proxy wraps an object behind the *same* interface to control access (lazy, remote, protected). Adapter wraps it behind a *different* interface; the visible signature changes, which is the Adapter signal.
